items.title: Epidemiology of Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease (ALD)
items.content: The prevalence and incidence of ALD are significantly influenced by alcohol consumption patterns. In the United States, unhealthy drinking affects approximately 7% of adults, with specific criteria for daily and episodic heavy drinking. The COVID-19 pandemic has exacerbated excessive alcohol intake, increasing morbidity and mortality from ALD. Prevalence rates indicate that 4.7% of the general population in the U.S. have alcohol-associated fatty liver disease, while 1.5% exhibit stage 2 or greater fibrosis. Alcohol is a major contributor to cirrhosis globally, accounting for 48% of cases and being the eleventh leading cause of mortality worldwide.
keyPoints: 7% of U.S. adults engage in unhealthy drinking (≥2 drinks/day for women, ≥3 drinks/day for men or heavy episodic drinking: ≥4 drinks for women, ≥5 drinks for men on a single occasion).
COVID-19 pandemic increased excessive alcohol intake.
Prevalence of alcohol-associated fatty liver disease is 4.7% in the U.S.; 1.5% have stage 2 or greater fibrosis.
48% of cirrhosis cases are due to alcohol; it's the eleventh leading cause of mortality worldwide, causing 1.16 million deaths annually.
Annual incidence rate for acute alcohol-associated hepatitis: 24-27 per million in women and 46-65 per million in men (European population).
items.reference: Alcohol Associated Liver Disease · Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Pathogenesis of Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease
items.content: Ethanol metabolism primarily occurs in the liver, where it is oxidized to acetaldehyde via alcohol dehydrogenase 1 (ADH1) and cytochrome P450 family 2 subfamily E member 1 (CYP2E1). Acetaldehyde's toxic effects, along with reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated during ethanol metabolism, cause hepatocyte injury through lipid peroxidation and DNA damage. The conversion of acetaldehyde to acetate by acetaldehyde dehydrogenase (ALDH) is crucial; ALDH2 deficiency leads to acetaldehyde accumulation, common in Asian populations, causing nausea and flushing. Alcohol induces hepatic steatosis via increased lipogenesis gene expression, suppression of fatty acid transport and oxidation genes, and altered NAD/NADH ratios reducing mitochondrial β-oxidation.
keyPoints: Ethanol is metabolized to acetaldehyde by ADH1 and CYP2E1 in the liver.
Acetaldehyde causes hepatocyte injury via lipid peroxidation and DNA damage.
ALDH2 deficiency leads to acetaldehyde accumulation, causing nausea and flushing (common in Asian populations).
Alcohol increases lipogenesis gene expression while suppressing fatty acid transport/oxidation genes.
Increased NAD/NADH ratio reduces mitochondrial β-oxidation, contributing to hepatic steatosis.
items.reference: Alcohol Associated Liver Disease · Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS FOR PROGRESSION OF ALD | Tiredness | TABLE 353-2 Symptoms and Signs Associated with Alcohol-Associated Cirrhosis and Alcohol-Associated Hepatitis
items.title: Risk Factors for Progression of Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease (ALD)
items.content: Progression from hepatic steatosis to cirrhosis in ALD is influenced by multiple factors beyond alcohol consumption. These include genetic predispositions, lifestyle choices such as smoking and drinking patterns, and coexisting metabolic conditions.
keyPoints: 10–20% of individuals with heavy drinking develop progressive liver disease and cirrhosis.
Women are at risk for ALD at lower daily alcohol intake compared to men.
Cigarette smoking is an independent risk factor for alcohol-associated cirrhosis.
Binge drinking and excessive alcohol consumption outside meals increase the risk of progressive ALD.
Obesity and MASLD frequently contribute to progression of ALD.
Genetic predisposition involves polymorphisms in PNPLA3, MBOAT7, TM6SF2, HSD17B13, and FAF2 genes.
items.reference: Alcohol Associated Liver Disease · RISK FACTORS FOR PROGRESSION OF ALD | Tiredness | TABLE 353-2 Symptoms and Signs Associated with Alcohol-Associated Cirrhosis and Alcohol-Associated Hepatitis

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS FOR PROGRESSION OF ALD | Tiredness | TABLE 353-2 Symptoms and Signs Associated with Alcohol-Associated Cirrhosis and Alcohol-Associated Hepatitis
items.title: Symptoms and Signs of Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease
items.content: Clinical manifestations of alcohol-associated cirrhosis and hepatitis include a range of systemic symptoms, skin changes, and neurologic signs. These are important for diagnosis and monitoring disease progression.
keyPoints: Skin: spider angioma, palmar erythema, jaundice, ecchymoses
Abdominal symptoms: discomfort, hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, caput medusae, ascites with weight gain
Neurologic signs: peripheral neuropathy, hepatic encephalopathy (asterixis), Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome
Other signs: icteric sclerae, rhinophyma, gynecomastia, gonadal atrophy
items.reference: Alcohol Associated Liver Disease · RISK FACTORS FOR PROGRESSION OF ALD | Tiredness | TABLE 353-2 Symptoms and Signs Associated with Alcohol-Associated Cirrhosis and Alcohol-Associated Hepatitis

items.title: Recognizing Decompensation in Alcohol-Associated Liver Cirrhosis
items.content: Patients with alcohol-associated liver cirrhosis often remain asymptomatic until decompensation occurs. Decompensation is marked by symptoms such as jaundice, fever, malaise, and signs of hepatic failure like ascites or encephalopathy.
keyPoints: Symptoms arise when patients with alcohol-associated liver cirrhosis decompensate.
Rapid onset of jaundice (serum bilirubin >3 mg/dL) is a key indicator.
12–26% of severe cases present with infections at admission.
Hepatorenal syndrome and SIRS are common in alcohol-associated hepatitis.
items.reference: Alcohol Associated Liver Disease · CLINICAL FEATURES | LABORATORY FINDINGS | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Laboratory Markers for Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease (ALD)
items.content: In ALD, liver enzyme ratios and specific thresholds help differentiate between conditions. Elevated AST with a higher ratio to ALT (>1) is characteristic of steatohepatitis.
keyPoints: AST/ALT ratio >1 indicates steatohepatitis; rarely AST >300 IU/L.
In alcohol-associated hepatitis, AST/ALT ratio >1.5 and serum bilirubin >3 mg/dL are typical.
Normal liver function tests can be seen in simple hepatic steatosis.
items.reference: Alcohol Associated Liver Disease · CLINICAL FEATURES | LABORATORY FINDINGS | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Diagnosing and Staging Alcohol-Associated Liver Fibrosis
items.content: Noninvasive techniques like transient elastography can stage liver fibrosis, while biopsy confirms the presence of specific histological features.
keyPoints: Liver stiffness <6 kPa indicates normal liver; >8 kPa suggests ≥F3 advanced fibrosis; >12.5 kPa indicates F4 cirrhosis.
Fib-4 score <3.25 can exclude advanced fibrosis or cirrhosis.
Histology shows perivenular fibrosis and septal fibrosis in alcohol-associated steatohepatitis.
items.reference: Alcohol Associated Liver Disease · CLINICAL FEATURES | LABORATORY FINDINGS | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Importance of Alcohol Abstinence in ALD Management
items.content: Prolonged alcohol abstinence is the most effective therapy to reduce progression and reverse conditions like hepatic steatosis and steatohepatitis associated with alcohol use.
keyPoints: Alcohol abstinence can reverse alcohol-associated hepatic steatosis and steatohepatitis.
No approved drugs exist for treating alcohol-associated steatosis or steatohepatitis with fibrosis.
items.reference: Alcohol Associated Liver Disease · TREATMENT | Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease (Fig. 353-1)

items.title: Criteria and Management of Severe Alcohol-Associated Hepatitis
items.content: Use the Maddrey discriminant function (MDF) or MELD score to assess severity; glucocorticoids are beneficial for patients with severe cases, but contraindications must be considered.
keyPoints: Severe alcohol-associated hepatitis is indicated by MDF ≥32 or MELD >20.
Glucocorticoid treatment reduces 28-day mortality risk in severe cases.
Contraindications for glucocorticoids include uncontrolled infections, AKI, and gastrointestinal bleeding.
items.reference: Alcohol Associated Liver Disease · TREATMENT | Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease (Fig. 353-1)

items.title: Liver Transplantation Considerations in ALD
items.content: Liver transplantation is a definitive therapy for patients with alcohol-associated decompensated cirrhosis or HCC, but requires a minimum of 6 months of abstinence.
keyPoints: Alcohol-associated liver disease is the leading indication for liver transplantation in the U.S.
Patients must demonstrate at least 6 months of alcohol abstinence before evaluation for transplantation.
Early liver transplantation may be considered for selected patients with severe, nonresponsive hepatitis.
items.reference: Alcohol Associated Liver Disease · TREATMENT | Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease (Fig. 353-1)

items.question: Which laboratory finding is characteristic of steatohepatitis?
options: Elevated levels of AST and GGT
items.explanation: Steatohepatitis is characterized by elevated levels of aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT).
items.reference: Alcohol Associated Liver Disease · CLINICAL FEATURES | LABORATORY FINDINGS | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the typical AST/ALT ratio in patients with alcohol-associated hepatitis?
items.explanation: Patients with alcohol-associated hepatitis have an AST/ALT ratio of >1.5.

items.question: What is the typical serum bilirubin level in patients presenting with alcohol-associated hepatitis?
items.explanation: Patients with alcohol-associated hepatitis often present with serum bilirubin >3 mg/dL.

items.question: Which imaging technique is rarely indicated for diagnosing alcohol-associated hepatic steatosis or steatohepatitis?
items.explanation: Liver biopsy is rarely indicated for diagnosing alcohol-associated hepatic steatosis or steatohepatitis.

items.question: Which of the following is currently not approved as a treatment for alcohol-associated steatosis and steatohepatitis?
items.explanation: There are currently no approved drugs for treatment of alcohol-associated steatosis and steatohepatitis.

items.question: What is the recommended frequency of HCC screening using liver ultrasonography in patients with cirrhosis?
items.explanation: HCC screening is recommended every 6 months using liver ultrasonography and serum AFP in patients with cirrhosis.

items.question: Which score can be used to predict short-term mortality in patients diagnosed with alcohol-associated hepatitis?
items.explanation: The Maddrey discriminant function (MDF) is used to predict short-term mortality in alcohol-associated hepatitis.

items.question: What is the preferred glucocorticoid treatment for patients with severe alcohol-associated hepatitis who can take oral medications?
items.explanation: Oral prednisolone, 40 mg/d for a total duration of 4 weeks, is preferred for patients able to take oral medications.

items.explanation: The passage indicates that chronic alcohol consumption induces the expression of CYP2E1.

items.explanation: Only 10–20% of those with daily alcohol consumption or heavy drinking will develop progressive liver disease.

items.statement: Glucocorticoid treatment reduces the risk of death in patients with severe alcohol-associated hepatitis beyond 6 months.

items.assertion: Genetic predisposition plays a role in the development of alcohol-associated liver cirrhosis.
items.reason: Twin studies have demonstrated a genetic predisposition to alcohol-associated liver cirrhosis that is independent from the genetic predisposition to alcohol use disorder.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Liver biopsy is always required to diagnose alcohol-associated hepatitis.
items.reason: In the presence of potential confounding factors, a transjugular liver biopsy may be recommended.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; liver biopsy is rarely indicated for diagnosing alcohol-associated hepatitis unless there are confounding factors.
